query: disadvantages of anti-scatter grids in radiography

retrieved_docs.title: Fluoroscopy Orthopedic Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Collimator: X-rays are emitted in all directions from the X-ray tube. Collimators are used to limit the X-ray field to a particular area and minimize spillover radiation out of the field of view. Unnecessarily large X-ray fields have two major disadvantages: 1) increased patient dose and 2) reduced image quality. Therefore, two sets of separately adjustable shutters are attached to the X-ray tube to limit the field. To help alignment, a light is used to form a field projecting an image of the X-ray field on the patient. Patient table: The table must be strong enough to support the patient but absorb less radiation to minimize over-exposure to obtain an adequate image. Grid plate: Anti scatter grids are common components of fluoroscopy and are used in procedures with high scatter. They consist of absorbing lead strips separated by transparent materials like paper. They minimize scatter of radiation and can be detached based on the requirement of the procedure.

retrieved_docs.title: Safety of Fluoroscopy in Patient, Operator, and Technician  -- Function -- X-Ray Basics, Design and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Collimation is also utilized to design the X-ray fluoroscopic machine, which helps to narrow and direct the primary X-ray beam out of the X-ray tube, usually via an iris-shaped lens. This decreases the amount of patient tissue exposed to scattered radiation, decreases occupational exposure to scattered radiation to healthcare personnel, and improves image quality. [2] Mounted to the X-ray tube is a separator cone, which serves as a barrier to prevent the patient's position from exceeding the minimum distance needed between the X-ray source and the patient skin. This serves to help protect patients from acute radiative skin injuries. Another design feature includes anti-scatter grids on the image intensifier, which decrease the amount of scattered radiation that reaches the entrance phosphor and ultimately increases image quality. However, when the anti-scatter grids are in place, it greatly increases the amount of radiation exposure required to generate an image. Therefore, it is ideally removed when conditions for scattering are low to decrease the amount of radiation delivered ultimately. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: X-Ray Image Quality Assurance -- Issues of Concern -- Antiscatter grid
retrieved_docs.content: An antiscatter grid improves image quality by decreasing scattered radiation. However, attenuating the primary X-ray beam can also negatively affect image quality. [3] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Production Technical Evaluation -- Issues of Concern -- Artifact
retrieved_docs.content: An artifact on an image is a feature that does not correlate with the physical properties of the subject being imaged and may confound or obscure the interpretation of that image. Because the mechanism for image creation is different between flat-panel detectors and computed radiography, the causes and appearances of some artifacts can be unique to these different modalities. Artifacts can occur for several reasons; examples include damage to X-ray detectors, liquid contamination of X-ray detectors, backscatter radiation detection, and image saturation. The typical appearances of the aforementioned artifacts on chest radiography are listed below. [17] Damage to X-ray detectors: White banding that can progressively grow over time Liquid contamination: Repeated vertical banding Backscatter radiation: Shadow image of superimposed digital detector electronics Image saturation: Clipping of anatomy

retrieved_docs.title: X-Ray Image Quality Assurance -- Definition/Introduction -- Artifacts
retrieved_docs.content: Artifacts contribute to poor image quality due to factors other than low resolution, noise, and SNR. These include unequal magnification, nonuniform images due to detector problems, bad detector elements, aliasing, and improper use of grids.

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Production Technical Evaluation -- Issues of Concern -- X-Ray Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: The collimator mentioned previously is supposed to help reduce the amount of scatter radiation in each performed radiograph, but there is always some degree of scatter radiation. Additional methods to help reduce scatter include the grid placed behind the patient and in front of the image cassette/receptor. Different scatter grids have different grid ratios: the height of the lead strips (H) divided by the space between the strips (D). The standard grid ratio is approximately 10, with 45 lines per centimeter of the scatter grid. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Image Acquisition -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Image Quality - Several factors determine image quality but are best characterized by a high signal-to-noise ratio (low noise) and good spatial resolution. Additional factors include factors relating to film processing, factors relating to geometric characteristics (such as magnification), and factors relating to the patient. The technologist has an important role in maintaining good image quality. For example, preventing patient movement helps reduce motion blur. [3] X-rays can either pass through a patient or are scattered by Compton interaction, which results in noise. Also, high kVp worsens the signal-to-noise ratio, while the thickness of the tissue being penetrated also increases noise. Using higher kVp results in an x-ray beam of higher average energy requiring a shorter exposure time and mA (together referred to as tube current exposure time product value or mAs), increasing patient penetration but also resulting in increased scatter and decreased image contrast. The converse is also true. A lower kVp setting requires increased mAs, resulting in decreased patient penetration, decreased scatter, and increased contrast. Carefully balancing these factors is essential for managing the relationship between patient dose, image contrast, and quality. Reduction of scatter radiation can be achieved by using grids, which have the added benefit of improving contrast and play an important role in producing an adequate quality and interpretable radiographic image. Although kVp increases noise, it also reduces patient dose. Collimators are devices that restrict/narrow beams and control scatters, resulting in increased signal-to-noise and decreased patient dose. In addition to image contrast, spatial resolution is an important measure of image quality. A smaller field of view results in a higher spatial resolution of the image and a decreased overall dose to the patient.

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Production Technical Evaluation -- Issues of Concern -- X-Ray Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: Compton scatter is when an X-ray photon hits an electron in the outer shell of an atom and produces a lower-energy scattered X-ray photon and an ionized electron, which is immediately knocked out of orbit. This scatter occurs at all energy levels but begins to predominate at higher kVps. Compton scattering is undesirable in medical imaging. It "fogs" the image because of photon scattering, causes biological damage to tissue, and is the reason for radiographer exposure to X-ray radiation. It also increases the patient's dose of radiation. Patients with larger body habitus are at increased risk for Compton scatter, as there is more soft tissue density for X-ray photons to travel through. Compton scattering is one reason it is so important for radiographers to understand and apply the concepts of mAs and kVp, as proper manipulation of these 2 values can significantly reduce the amount of Compton scatter in a radiograph. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: X-Ray Image Quality Assurance -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Appropriately using effective collimation and an antiscatter grid reduces scattered radiation and improves image quality by reducing noise and improving SNR. The anti-scatter grid is most useful when the amount of scattered radiation is high, especially if the patient's thickness is greater than 10 cm.[3] However, an antiscatter grid is not useful in smaller or pediatric patients or for smaller body part imaging.

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Image Production Procedures -- Issues of Concern -- Contrast
retrieved_docs.content: Determining anatomy and suspected pathologies effectively requires identifying and separating different tissue types and boundaries. In X-ray imaging, contrast describes the number of relative photons that can pass through a tissue compared to another. This is determined by the amount of tube voltage (kVp) and filtration used. Conversely, increasing the mA does not improve or worsen contrast and contributes to the image's noise. [14] Selecting parameters with lower kVp allows for the best separation in a given spectrum of intensities and improves contrast. However, this is always balanced with achieving enough exposure and penetration. Another technique to improve contrast to noise is using a grid to reduce scatter. [9] The choice of the grid is based on imaging modality (breast, abdomen, skull), and grid spacing ratios contribute to the reduction of noise.

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Production Technical Evaluation -- Issues of Concern -- X-Ray Production
retrieved_docs.content: Collimation is "focusing" an X-ray beam on a specific target using lead plates. The X-rays shot from the beam move through the collimator, and the plates absorb any X-rays that are not parallel with the lead plates. This produces X-rays that increase the spatial resolution of the resultant radiograph. Proper collimation is important, as X-ray beams that are over-collimated may hide diagnostically useful details, and under-collimation can expose extra radiation to patients and result in increased scatter radiation. The collimated X-rays then travel through the patient, the lead grid behind the patient (which only allows parallel X-rays to pass), and onto the imaging cassette. In summary, an X-ray tube produces an X-ray beam, travels through a lead filter, is collimated, passes through the patient, passes through a lead grid, and then hits the imaging cassette to produce a radiograph. [2] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Nuclear Medicine Artifacts -- Issues of Concern -- Instrumental Artifacts
retrieved_docs.content: Field-of-view artifacts can result from various mechanisms. Electronic masking of the unusable edges helps prevent edge-packing effects along the crystal area. However, this can reduce the field of view due to overcompensation or misalignment, leaving edge effects visible. These artifacts can be prevented through proper calibration after servicing the machine. Misalignment can introduce errors in any series of static images. When multiple passes are used for acquisition, a "zipper" (linear) artifact may appear, while continuous acquisition may result in banding, often seen as horizontal linear artifacts. This can be prevented through proper equipment maintenance to ensure whole-body uniformity. Hard copies of images should be avoided, as artifacts related to the printing process and damage to the print medium are common. Truncation errors can also occur when the PET and CT fields of view differ, when patients are positioned away from the in-plane center, or when the patient's size exceeds the field of view, although software can correct most of these issues. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Fluoroscopy Orthopedic Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The intensifier must be as close to the patient as feasible. This prevents the beam from dispersing and reduces the radiation required for a sharp image. Removal of the anti-scatter grid decreases the total radiation dose but with a slight increase in the amount of radiation scatter. A balance must be attained between the total dosage and the amount of scatter. Pulsed fluoroscopy reduces fluoroscopy time by 76% and radiation dose by 64% compared with continuous fluoroscopy. Decreasing the frame rate further decreases the effective radiation. Collimation must be used whenever feasible. [9] A reduced field of view (FOV) decreases scatter and decreases the exposure of the patient to unwanted parts.

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Production Technical Evaluation -- Issues of Concern -- X-Ray Production
retrieved_docs.content: The accumulation of electrons at the cathode results in a large negative charge that accelerates electrons toward the anode target. The cathode of an X-ray tube is made of a tungsten filament. X-ray tubes tend to have 2 pieces of tungsten per anode (dual-focus tubes), one measuring 1 cm in length and the second measuring 2 cm in length. The smaller the filament, the smaller the current the filament can handle and the fewer electrons the filament can produce. The smaller filament is used to take radiographs of smaller body parts. The larger filament is used for imaging of larger body parts. Because of the increased amount of electrons in the beam, there is increased repulsion amongst the beam's electrons, creating scattering of the beam and decreased spatial resolution of radiographs. This is corrected by having a negatively charged focusing cap at the end of the cathode, which repels the electrons away from the peripheral areas and creates a very narrow, focused electron beam, increasing spatial resolution. Now that the electrons have been excited and emitted from the tungsten filaments, they are accelerated toward the positively charged anode.

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Stress Imaging -- Indications -- Disadvantages
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation exposure, with radioactivity for 3 to 4 days post-procedure Diagnostic accuracy reduced by arrhythmias and soft-tissue attenuation [5] 300 lbs weight limit for most machines Severe claustrophobia may prohibit use [8]

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Image Acquisition -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Digital Radiography - The computed radiographic image receptor, an elementary form of a digital detector, is similar yet different from screen-film imaging in a few ways. One similarity is their mutual use of X-ray-sensitive plates. Computed radiography using storage phosphors uses trapped electrons in metastable states to produce an image instead of a scintillator used in screen-film radiography. Metastable electrons are released with a stimulating laser beam, with the emission of light on the return of these electrons to a ground state. [11] This light emission is used to establish an image. Computed radiography also comparatively has a reduced radiation dose and better contrast resolution when compared to screen radiography (although the resolution is worse in digital radiography). Flat Panel Detectors are classically associated with digital detectors. It is faster than film radiography and includes direct and indirect systems. Explained simply, indirect systems require a latent image to be released as a light, then converted to a charge and a digital image. In contrast, direct systems directly convert x-rays into charge (and subsequently an image) without this intermediate step. Indirect systems employ a cesium iodide scintillator, which constitutes most flat-panel detectors, while direct systems employ amorphous selenium.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1491
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray is a fundamental radiographic examination that utilizes plain X‑ray techniques as its method of acquisition. Building on this basic imaging approach, more advanced studies are conceptually derived from the routine chest x-ray. A computerized tomography of chest is directly mapped from the routine chest x-ray as its primary extension, providing cross‑sectional detail of thoracic structures. Additionally, a computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium represents another, more comprehensive variant that is also mapped from the routine chest x-ray, offering combined neuro‑and thoracic evaluation with enhanced contrast visualization. This hierarchy illustrates how basic plain X‑ray methods underpin increasingly sophisticated CT imaging modalities, guiding clinical assessment and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2366
retrieved_docs.content: Plain X-ray techniques encompass tomografie as a subordinate imaging method, indicating that tomografie is classified as a child technique within the broader category of plain X-ray imaging. This relationship highlights that tomografie derives from or is considered a specialized extension of standard plain X-ray procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2519
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a basic imaging modality, and both computerized tomography of brain and chest and computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium are considered alternative or mapped imaging studies derived from the routine chest x-ray, indicating that these CT examinations may be employed for more detailed evaluation when the findings from a standard chest x-ray are insufficient.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2131
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a reference point for several more advanced computed tomography examinations. Both Computerized tomography of brain and chest and Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium are considered alternative mappings derived from a routine chest x-ray, indicating that these comprehensive CT studies may be ordered when a standard chest x-ray is insufficient. Additionally, Computerized tomography of chest is identified as the primary mapping from a routine chest x-ray, reflecting its role as the most direct CT extension of the basic chest radiograph. Understanding these relationships helps clinicians select appropriate imaging modalities, progressing from a routine chest x-ray to targeted CT imaging of the chest alone or combined brain‑chest assessment, with or without contrast, based on diagnostic needs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 291
retrieved_docs.content: Tomografie is recognized as a medical diagnostic imaging modality; it is classified within the broader category of plain X‑ray techniques and is considered a specific type of medical diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 1218
retrieved_docs.content: CT angiography of thorax with contrast, Computerized axial tomography of thorax with contrast, and the two diagnostic computed tomography procedures of the thorax—one performed without contrast material and another performed without contrast material followed by contrast material and additional sections—are all specific modalities that fall under the broader category of a chest CT scan. These relationships indicate that each of these named CT techniques is a subtype (“isa”) or a more narrowly defined variant of the general “ct scan chest” procedure, reflecting differences in contrast use and imaging phases. In addition, the imaging entry labeled “Other chest x‑ray” is linked to the Arabic‑language term “التصوير المقطعي المحوسب للصدر” (computed tomography of the chest) as a parent concept, positioning this x‑ray study within the same overarching chest imaging hierarchy. Collectively, the hierarchy demonstrates that various CT thorax protocols—whether with contrast, without contrast, or with combined phases—are organized as specialized forms of chest CT, while other chest radiographic examinations are considered related imaging modalities within the same parent imaging domain. This structured classification aids clinicians in selecting the appropriate chest imaging technique based on diagnostic needs and contrast requirements.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2416
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a foundational imaging reference for several computed tomography (CT) examinations. It is the primary source from which a dedicated Computerized tomography of chest is mapped, indicating a direct relationship between the standard chest radiograph and the more detailed CT chest study. In addition, the routine chest x-ray is also linked, through other mappings, to more extensive CT examinations that combine multiple anatomical regions: Computerized tomography of brain and chest, Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium, and Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen with intravenous contrast medium. These connections illustrate that the routine chest x-ray not only underpins isolated chest CT imaging but also informs broader, multi‑region CT protocols, both with and without the use of intravenous contrast, highlighting its central role in guiding advanced diagnostic imaging strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1990
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray is used as a reference point for several advanced computed tomography (CT) examinations. The CT scan of the chest is primarily mapped from the routine chest x‑ray, indicating it is the main derivative imaging study. In addition, CT examinations that include the brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium, as well as CT scans encompassing the brain, chest, and abdomen, are also linked to the routine chest x‑ray through other mappings, reflecting supplementary or alternative relationships. These connections illustrate how a basic chest radiograph can serve as a foundational study for more comprehensive, multi‑region CT imaging, sometimes enhanced with contrast agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 383
retrieved_docs.content: how a simple chest X‑ray can serve as the gateway to a hierarchy of imaging techniques—from plain radiography to advanced CT of multiple body regions—while also connecting to specific disease associations and broader neuro‑imaging frameworks essential for comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2728
retrieved_docs.content: The routine chest x-ray is used as a reference point for more extensive imaging examinations, with both computerized tomography of the brain and chest and computerized tomography of the brain, chest, and abdomen being considered alternative or mapped extensions of the routine chest x-ray. This relationship highlights how a basic chest radiograph can serve as a basis for selecting broader CT studies that include additional anatomical regions such as the brain, chest, and abdomen, informing clinical decisions about when more detailed cross‑sectional imaging may be warranted.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2375
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray is performed using plain X‑ray techniques. Computerized tomography of chest is considered the primary imaging modality that is mapped from the routine chest x‑ray, indicating that chest CT often follows or expands upon findings from a standard chest radiograph. In addition, computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen is another imaging study that is also mapped from the routine chest x‑ray, representing an alternative or supplementary advanced imaging approach that can be pursued when a broader anatomic assessment is required. These relationships illustrate how plain X‑ray techniques underlie routine chest x‑ray, which in turn serves as a reference point for both focused chest CT and more extensive CT examinations of the brain, chest, and abdomen.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 68
retrieved_docs.content: MISCELLANEOUS DIAGNOSTIC AND THERAPEUTIC PROCEDURES is the top‑level category that aggregates a broad spectrum of imaging and interventional modalities, including routine chest X‑ray (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر), magnetic resonance imaging (التصوير بالرنين المغناطيسي, MRI), ultrasound examination (فحص بالموجات فوق الصوتية), ophthalmologic and otologic diagnosis and treatment, and a wide array of radiographic and nuclear‑medicine procedures. Within this hierarchy, the sub‑category Other diagnostic radiology and related techniques organizes detailed radiologic services such as rentgenografie (radiography), tomografie (tomography), Other x‑ray, Soft tissue X‑ray of abdomen, pelvimetry, retrograde urography (تصوير الجهاز البولي بالطريق الراجع), Other nephrotomogram, and unlisted computed tomography procedures, maintaining inverse‑ISA links that designate rentgenografie as a specific form of radiographic imaging. MRI is cross‑referenced as a child of Diagnosis in the PsycINFO subcluster,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 446
retrieved_docs.content: CT modality; this Arabic‑named modality serves as a parent category for Other chest X‑ray, indicating that other chest X‑ray procedures are sub‑modalities under chest CT. Together, these relationships demonstrate how CT and X‑ray modalities are organized within a structured taxonomy that spans chest, abdominal, pelvic, and genitourinary imaging, supporting method selection and clinical interpretation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 385
retrieved_docs.content: CT follow-up is classified as a type of Other diagnostic radiology and related techniques, a category that also encompasses procedures such as Other x‑ray, Soft tissue X‑ray of abdomen, and Unlisted computed tomography procedures (e.g., diagnostic or interventional). The broader term rentgenografie serves as an overarching parent for Other diagnostic radiology and related techniques. In practice, CT follow‑up is performed as a method of Computed tomography imaging – action, and this imaging action itself functions as a method for carrying out unlisted computed tomography procedures. Together, these relationships delineate how CT follow‑up fits within the hierarchy of radiologic techniques, links specific imaging actions to broader procedural categories, and aligns with the general field of rentgenografie.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1938
retrieved_docs.content: CT of chest, abdomen and pelvis, Computerized tomography of chest, and Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen (both with and without intravenous contrast medium) are imaging procedures that generate a computerized tomographic image (صورة مقطعية محوسبة). Each of these CT examinations is linked to the routine chest x‑ray as a source reference: the CT of chest, abdomen and pelvis and the Computerized tomography of chest are primarily mapped from the routine chest x‑ray, indicating a direct derivation or extension of the standard chest radiograph, whereas the Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen and its contrast‑enhanced variant are mapped from the routine chest x‑ray in an “other” capacity, reflecting an alternative or supplementary relationship. This mapping underscores the routine chest x‑ray’s role as a foundational imaging study that can be expanded into more detailed cross‑sectional CT assessments, all of which produce the same type of output—a computerized tomographic image (صورة مقطعية محوسبة).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 862
retrieved_docs.content: Chest imaging encompasses several distinct modalities that are organized hierarchically. The broad category “Computerized axial tomography (CT) scan” serves as a parent for chest‑specific computed tomography, referred to in Arabic as “التصوير المقطعي المحوسب للصدر” (computed tomography of the chest). Within this chest CT family, there are multiple specialized procedures. “CT angiography of thorax with contrast” and “Computerized axial tomography of thorax with contrast” are both defined as specific types (“isa”) of chest CT scans, indicating that they are contrast‑enhanced CT examinations of the thorax. Diagnostic thoracic CT performed without contrast material, as well as the same study followed by contrast administration and additional sections, are described as narrower subcategories of chest CT, reflecting variations in contrast use and imaging protocol. Although the list also links “Other chest x‑ray” to the chest CT category and to the Arabic chest‑CT term, radiographically a chest x‑ray is a separate imaging modality and should be regarded as a sibling rather than a child of chest CT. Overall, the knowledge structure clarifies that chest CT includes both non‑contrast and contrast‑enhanced techniques, with specific angiographic and diagnostic variants, while chest x‑ray remains a distinct, parallel imaging option.
